title: Share who are worried about vs. share of deaths from terrorism
subtitle: Respondents who said they worried "very much" or "a great deal". Confirmed deaths, including all victims and attackers who died because of a [terrorist attack](#dod:terrorism).
caption:
Share who are worried about vs. share of deaths from terrorism
Respondents who said they worried "very much" or "a great deal". Confirmed deaths, including all victims and attackers who died because of a terrorist attack.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of people worried about terrorism and the share of deaths from terrorism across various countries, with circle sizes representing the number of attacks. The vertical axis measures the share of people worried about terrorism, ranging from 30% to 90%, while the horizontal axis displays the share of deaths from terrorism on a logarithmic scale from 0.0001% to 1%. Countries are color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. Myanmar and Nigeria show high levels of worry, both above 90%, despite having relatively low shares of deaths from terrorism, around 0.01% and 0.1% respectively. Iraq stands out with a high share of deaths from terrorism, near 1%, and a worry level around 85%. In contrast, New Zealand has one of the lowest worry levels, around 35%, and a low share of deaths from terrorism, approximately 0.1%. European countries like Germany and the United Kingdom cluster in the lower-left quadrant, with worry levels around 60% and 50% respectively, and very low shares of deaths from terrorism, below 0.001%. Asian countries such as India and Thailand show moderate worry levels, around 60% and 50%, with low shares of deaths from terrorism, near 0.01%. The data shows no clear linear relationship between the share of people worried about terrorism and the share of deaths from terrorism, as countries with high worry levels do not necessarily have high shares of deaths from terrorism, and vice versa.
